speaker: interviewer
text: By Nathan Lipschitz. And today is January 26, 1996.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my picture, my wife's picture, when we-- right after we got engaged. As you can see, I didn't have the beard. I was-- it took me quite a number, quite a few years, until I changed, and I let my beard grow back, back to the Hasidic outfit. It wasn't any revolutionary in me. But I was-- the circumstances were that it took me a little time because I was working in different places that I thought it wasn't appropriate yet to wear those, those very Hasidic clothes. But after I got married, I started all over again, going back to the Hasidic clothes, and let's grow the beard. That's the family again, with my son in Israel, my oldest son, who lives in Israel. And we were there, we took the picture. It's my wife and me, my daughter Kazy and my granddaughter Dasi, and Miri. And the other kid, the little one, isn't here. And my daughter-in-law is not here either. I don't know if it was a reason, but she wasn't there probably when we took the picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say about at least five, six years ago. Well, this is my wife, Claire Lipschitz.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were married about 40 years with Mazel. And her maiden name was Skolnik. And we are happily married, at least we have to say so. [LAUGHTER] And, and my side, in the back of me is my son, Yitzhak, in English, Ira.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they are married, what, about--

speaker: interviewee
text: 14 years. And happily married. And they have lovely four children.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm going to be 11 in March.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, 11 years in March. And she's a good girl in school, very good. And she is a, a, real, real good friend to her friends and has a good keppel on herself.

speaker: interviewee
text: And can I put a word in too?

speaker: interviewee
text: --Joshie. The only son so far in the family.

speaker: interviewee
text: So far is right. And he is? How old are you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nine years old. There might be a contradiction to what I told you before, but--

speaker: interviewee
text: I hope it's forgiven. It I think I said eight.

speaker: interviewee
text: All right, and he's a very good boy, too. I really mean it. They're both wonderful kids.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he's Elezer Yeshayu, named after your brother.

speaker: interviewee
text: He's Elezer Yeshayu, named after my brother, Joshie is in English.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's for him. Now, he is supposed to be the continuation of that dynasty. Now, since yesterday, he has competition.

speaker: interviewee
text: My other son in Israel, thank god, gave, he gave birth to-- not the son, the wife-- [LAUGHTER] --Gave birth to a boy.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we have [YIDDISH] Hindala. She's named after my sister.

speaker: interviewee
text: Wave, wave, put your hand up.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hinda Ayala in English. And she is a wonderful, good, smart girl. And she has very artistic talents.

speaker: interviewee
text: And you say she looks like your sister.

speaker: interviewee
text: They all have-- and she looks a lot like my sister, too. And she's also good in school and good in the house.

speaker: interviewee
text: Number four, finally, the cutie pie.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- oh, Danni. And she is, what?

speaker: interviewee
text: Six years old. Also a very good girl, smart. And what else? I mean, everything that the father sees in their grandchild that he loves-- very good in school and smart and a good kid. And then this is my son's family. And then I have my [YIDDISH]. Her name is [YIDDISH] Baile in Yiddish, [YIDDISH]. In English is Karen.

speaker: interviewee
text: We'll stick that in now. Karen.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she is no more teenager. She's already past her 20. And she is our apple of the eye.

speaker: interviewee
text: Of our eye. And we are happy with her in the house. But some day we will have to lose her for a while to be out and just to visit her, and they'll visit us. But we wait for that day. And pretty soon it will come. We have a lot of naches, thank god, from her. She is smart, she is good. She is, she is everything that a parent could wish for.

speaker: interviewee
text: Do you remember, Ira, when we went to Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: And Daddy and Mommy took us back?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember it was so fascinating. Daddy had told us as we were growing up all about the shtetl and the shtetl life and the rebba's house and the shul and about all his brothers and sisters. It was sad that of course, we couldn't meet them, I'm sorry, because they were killed in the Holocaust. But when we went back to Wielepole, it was just this incredibly emotional experience. We got to see the actual-- we were standing on the actual ground where he was raised.

speaker: interviewee
text: After growing up with all the stories.

speaker: interviewee
text: They saw it in the spot.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was very, very emotional.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was nice. I just think after Steven Spielberg sees this, they should make a movie out of my father's life. [LAUGHTER] And you know, we can all star in it. And we'll become famous.

speaker: interviewee
text: But unfortunately my other son is not here. I don't know, unfortunately that we are not together. But he's happy. He lives in Israel. And as mentioned, his wife just gave birth. After three girls, they gave birth to a boy. And it's [YIDDISH] and happiness all around. And we hope to meet them soon.

speaker: interviewee
text: And have a lot of naches.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this might sound like a cheap commercial. But I really feel what Steven is doing is just incredible. And it will be something that will be forever.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyone have something to say to Zadie or something?

speaker: interviewee
text: How about you, Esther? Oh, you better not. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewee
text: You don't trust me to say something?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, to tell you the truth, I never thought I would marry into a family like this. I never dreamed I would have a father-in-law who looks like this. I grew up more-- I was orthodox. But I grew up in a more modern family. And to me, this is just a total surprise. But I'm not sorry for one second.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, wait a minute. Keep in mind that I grew up, and we grew up like this also.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's true. And my father changed-- That's true.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. And I did meet him before I met my father, father-in-law, so.

speaker: interviewee
text: Otherwise, it may have ended up different.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. So I'm not sorry. I'm so happy to be part of this family. They're wonderful. And I love them very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you very much on behalf of the Shoah Foundation.
